data.title: Hamburg Hafen: A Strong Sell After MSC Tender
data.description:
With global trade now being the center of attention due to tariffs, it is an interesting time to expand coverage with names that have exposure to trade. In this report, I will be adding Hamburger Hafen und Logistik AG (OTC:HHULF) (OTCPK:HHULY) (hereafter HHLA) to my coverage and explain why I believe it is best to sell the stock.

HHLA Is More Than Just The Port Of Hamburg

The port of Hamburg is the 21st busiest port in the world, the fourth busiest port in Europe and the busiest port in Germany. So, it plays a significant role in the transportation of goods. The company is not just about the harbor of Hamburg, even though that obviously is the center of the company. HHLA also has seaport terminals in Tallinn, Triest and Odessa. The company also has an intermodal network including intermodal terminals in the form of Metrans and in 2024 it acquired a majority stake in Roland Spedition furthering strengthening the intermodal network.

So, the company offers more than just container terminals and container handling in Hamburg as it also has an intermodal network, sea terminals at other ports, provides container maintenance and repair, intermodal loading and unloading, intermodal terminals, airborne logistics, consulting and real estate solutions in the warehouse district of Hamburg. The company is 66.8% owned by Hamburg through class A shares with MSC holding a strategic stake of 23.4% and 6.3% is free float as of the September 30, 2024.

A Challenging Environment For HHLA

The environment for HHLA currently is a rather challenging one. Revenues grew 8.9% driven by 8.9% growth in transport volumes while throughput at the port remained more or less flat but had a tailwind from temporary higher storage fees. The higher transport volumes are the result of the acquisition of a 51% stake in Roland Spedition in Q2. EBIT increased 31.8% to €81.5 million, putting the EBIT margins at 7.1% marking a 1.3 percent increase.

The Container segment saw revenues increase by 8.2% to €578.1 million, driven by higher storage fees and a 0.9% increase in throughput. This resulted in EBIT increasing 87.1% to €51 million on a more favorable revenue mix. The intermodal segment revenues increased 12% to €521.9 million driven by strong growth in rail volumes and pricing, but the EBIT increased just 2.2% to €62.7 million due to higher labor costs, the floodings in Eastern Europe last year, a strike at the Hamburg terminals in Q3 and a shift in the cargo mix. In the Logistics segment, revenues increased to €60.2 million, marking a 1.9% increase but EBIT losses increased from €0.8 million to €1 million due to lower results at the vehicle logistics business which had a strong year last year, partially offset by favorable leasing income.

The company also has reported preliminary figures for 2024, which show a 10.5% increase in revenues to €1.6 billion and a 22.7% increase in EBIT to €134 million, driven by higher temporary fees and strong growth in container transport through its network.

What Are The Risks And Opportunities For HHLA?

The obvious opportunity for HHLA is the long-term upward demand trend for transportation of goods and the company has opportunities to increase its profitability through automation, which means it can to some extent decouple volume growth from labor cost growth. At the same time, there are risks, and we pretty much see those risks developing right now. The German economy is rather weak at the moment. It should be noted that Hamburg does not have a huge exposure to German car exports, but the company still plays an important role in the German economy and if the economy is weak, there is a pressure on HHLA as well. Furthermore, global conflicts and trade conflicts also have an impact. The company saw more transport flows to and from the US after the elections in anticipation of potential tariffs. If tariffs are applied, we could see a reduction in trade volumes and if tariffs are not applied, we could see a normalization in the flow volumes. Furthermore, HHLA's higher revenues were largely supported by temporary higher fees, which were implemented due to higher container dwell times (time a container spent at a particular location). Once these elevated charges disappear, the company’s growth will hinge more on volume growth and regular rate adjustments.

I Believe Hamburg Hafen Stock Is A Sell

Particular to the stock, we note that the OTC listings (HHULF, HHULY) have no meaningful trading volumes, making buying and selling at desired prices and quantities difficult or nearly impossible. As a solution, one might want to consider buying directly from the Stock Exchange in Frankfurt where it is listed under the ticker HHFA, but it should be noted that volumes are not high on the home exchange either.

MSC and HHLA agreed in 2023 to operate the port as a joint-venture and made and MSC made a tender offer for the publicly traded shares at a price of €16.75. After the tender offer, around 6.3% of the shares were not tendered and those trade at around €18.50. The ADRs were not part of the tender offer and had to be converted to HHFA shares in order to be tendered.

As a result, given that the home listing also shows declining trading volumes as well as the uncertain outlook for the port, I do believe it is better to sell shares in the OTC listing as well as the home listing before trading volumes decline even further. Since MSC and the city of Hamburg operate the port as a joint-venture, there are little to no chances that a squeeze out will happen, but MSC has also not made a new offer for the remaining shares. So, I don’t see any appealing investment case here and mark the stock a strong sell as the free float is small, and the underlying investment case is weak at best.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SHO: The Preferred Stocks' 7.3% Yield Outshines The FFO Yield
data.description:
Financial thinking is always about choosing the better alternative. When you look at a particular company, you need to examine all the possible ways to invest in the company's assets. Today, we are diving into one of the safest Hotel REITs we've found, Sunstone Hotel Investors Inc. (NYSE:SHO). The company has two exchange-traded preferred stocks outstanding:

  • The 6.125% cumulative (NYSE:SHO.PR.H)
  • The 5.70% cumulative (NYSE:SHO.PR.I)

We will examine the best risk-adjusted option at the moment. SHO has just posted its fourth-quarter results, and we will use them as a guide when we compare preferred stock and equity returns. We will also examine where the preferred stocks stand in the fixed-income universe.

Company overview:

Our mission is to be the premier stewards of capital in the lodging industry, providing superior returns to our shareholders by investing in hotels where we can add value through capital investment, hotel repositioning, and asset management. In addition, we seek to capitalize on our portfolio's embedded value and balance sheet strength to actively recycle past investments into new growth and value creation opportunities in order to deliver strong shareholder returns and superior per-share NAV growth.

-- Company's website

To get more familiar with the company, you can see their properties in the picture below:

You can make a reservation directly from their website, but that is definitely not the purpose of this article. What we care about are good old numbers.

Financial information:

The balance sheet value of SHO's hotels is $2.86 billion. Total assets are around $3.1 billion. The company's debt is around $0.84 billion. Preferred stocks represent around $0.28 billion. The common stock trades at 1.23 times book value. If we use the market-adjusted value of the assets, we can boldly claim that the company's assets coverage ratio is close to 400%. This makes SHO one of the safest companies in the property REIT sector. The financial managers of SHO like unencumbered debt :

New Unsecured Term Loan and JW Marriott New Orleans Mortgage Repayment. In the fourth quarter of 2024, the Company entered into a new $100.0 million term loan agreement and used substantially all of the proceeds to repay the $72.1 million loan secured by the JW Marriott New Orleans. Following the repayment of the JW Marriott New Orleans loan, all of the Company's hotels are unencumbered and, inclusive of extension options, the Company has no debt maturities prior to 2026.

This adds exceptional flexibility in difficult times. Every time we research SHO, its credibility shines: low leverage, no secured debt, and a market-adjusted asset value higher than the book value.

Profitability:

SHO earns about 19% gross on renting its rooms. 26% of this revenue goes to cover room-related expenses. Excluding depreciation and amortization, operating expenses represent around 77% of revenues. We can approximate an asset's yield ((total revenue - operating expenses + depreciation and amortization) / total assets) of around 7.3% — unfortunately, SHO trades at 1.23 times its book value. If we use the same methodology to calculate the market-adjusted asset's yield, we receive 6.22%. Interest expenses for the year were close to $50 million. This is close to 5.9%. When you buy SHO common stock at its current market price, you purchase an asset earning 6.2%, leveraged around 0.25 times at 5.9%. If we use the adjusted FFO number from SHO's report:

We get an FFO yield per share of 0.8 / 11.3 or 6.3%. SHO has also posted guidance for the entire year of 2025:

If we are to use their numbers, the expected FFO yield is between 7.6% and 8.7%. Based on their operating margin, they will have to experience a slight improvement in numbers to meet the guidance. Common stockholders are always playing the taking-the-risk game. With hotel REITs, one cannot expect large upside deviations and positive surprises in performance. You will definitely not wake up tomorrow to see SHO common stock being 200% higher on a positive earnings report. Common stock returns in property REITs are similar to those of fixed income, with a slight upside but more risk. If we assume that management expects close to 8% FFO yield of its common stock, it is a good idea to look at what the preferred stock has to offer.

Preferred stocks:

The company's two exchange-traded stocks have current yields of around 7.3%, between its last reported yearly FFO yield and its expected FFO yield for the following year. The dividends of all preferred stocks in the company are around $15 million yearly. This is close to 10% of FFO. A tiny amount, even if management is completely wrong in its expectations. In terms of credibility, even if we add the preferred stocks to the company's overall leverage, The market-adjusted asset coverage ratio is above 300%. This is far greater than the leverage limit for BDCs and CEFs under the Securities Act of 1940. The fact that SHO trades above book value means that the coverage ratio is quite large, even in a fire sale. We have always been fans of SHO-preferred stocks and monitor them for any deviation from their place in the yield curve.

Historical pricing of SHO preferreds:

The two preferred stocks were issued back in 2021 at spreads to the 20-year Treasury at around 3.75% on average. At the time of writing, their spread to the 20-year is around 2.5%. They have narrowed their nominal spread, which has been the normal behavior for all high-quality preferred stocks for the last 4 years. Statistically, they don't represent a bargain compared to their historical valuations. The value of their embedded call option has decreased (they trade further below par), but this is not a good enough reason to justify such a narrowing of their credit spread. If we compare SHO-preferred stocks to similarly priced ones from the REIT sector:

We can see that SHO preferreds receive a "B+" rating from the market. This is a bit harsh for a hotel REIT that easily survived COVID-19. It's hard to imagine anything worse for a Hotel REIT.

Returns comparison:

If we assume that SHO common stock is to earn its 8% FFO yield going forward and take a 10-year horizon, we need to gain some extra 0.7% capital appreciation for the preferred stocks as we advance. This can happen either if the market decides to narrow the risk premium for the preferred stocks of SHO or by simply having long-term yields going lower in the future. We estimate that SHO is capable of getting a higher credit rating than B+ for its preferred stocks and view this as the most likely event to see even narrower spreads.

Conclusion:

If you are incredibly bullish on hotel prices in the future, SHO is among the best investments you can make; if you look at SHO as a business and an income investment, the preferred stock and their 7.3% outshine the common stock at the moment. The valuation can change at any moment, so it is good to pay attention to the spread of the expected FFO yield to the Current yield of the preferred stock.

data.title: American Homes 4 Rent: Preferred Shares Offer Better Value As NOI Growth Moderates
data.description:
Introduction

Over the past year, American Homes 4 Rent (NYSE:AMH) has marginally underperformed the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ), delivering a total return of ~7%, only half of the ~14% gain for the broad U.S. REIT ETF:

American Homes 4 Rent delivered solid Q4 2024 results with Core FFO growth driven by very healthy NOI expansion. Taking account of my expectations for moderating NOI growth in 2025-2026 and the elevated Core FFO multiple/low-market implied cap rate the common AMH shares trade at, I think they are currently worth a Hold rating. Instead, I make the case that the REIT's preferred shares currently offer better value, ranking them a Buy.

Company Overview

You can access all AMH results on its website here. American Homes 4 Rent is a residential REIT focused on single-family rental homes. The company's portfolio totals 60,531 properties, with the largest markets being Atlanta, GA (10% of all properties), Charlotte, NC (7%), and Dallas-Fort Worth, TX (6.4%):

The company's enterprise value totals $20 billion of which 75% is funded with equity, 24% with net debt, and only 1% with preferred shares.

Q4 2024 Results Overview

In Q4 2024 AMH delivered Core FFO of $0.45/share, up 4.9% Y/Y, taking 2024 Core FFO to $1.77/share - an increase of 6.6% relative to 2023. The year-over-year improvement was partially driven by higher Core NOI margins (66% in Q4 2024 and 64.9% in 2024). Core NOI growth came in at 8.5% Y/Y in Q4 2024 and 8.1% for full-year 2024, well ahead of the 1.8% increase in shares outstanding (as with many large REITs the company continues to issue shares to fund growth and maintain modest leverage). The strong NOI performance was primarily the result of higher rents, with the average monthly realized rent per property up 4.7% Y/Y in Q4 2024, marginally below the 5.3% increase for the full year 2024.

In contrast to the strong financial performance, the total average occupied days percentage (the REIT's key occupancy metric) stood at 94.2% at the end of Q4 2024, down 0.9% Q/Q and 0.8% Y/Y respectively. That said, the net result of higher rent and lower occupancy was clearly positive, with core revenues increasing 4% Y/Y in Q4 2024, and 5% for the full year 2024.

2025 Outlook

The company expects Core FFO growth to moderate to about 3.4% in 2025, driven by marginally lower margins (expense growth will likely outpace revenues) and a normalizing Core NOI growth of ~3%:

The guidance most notably anticipates a recovery in the total average occupied days percentage to ~96% but weaker rent growth marginally below 4%.

Overall I can conclude that 2025 is likely to see a moderation in NOI growth to a level closer to inflation. That said, the expected uptick in occupancy and the still strong rent increase for renewals (+4.9% Y/Y in Q4 2024) indicates the REIT will likely continue outperforming the inflation rate.

Valuation

American Homes 4 Rent ended 2024 with a Core NOI of $978 million, with the anticipated 3% growth rate indicating a level closer to $1.01 billion in 2025. Against the circa $20 billion in enterprise value, this implies the company trades at a market-implied cap rate of about 5% which is on the low end relative to U.S. residential REITs (especially smaller ones). General and administrative expenses totaled $83.6 million in 2024, or 8.5% of 2024 NOI, indicating the "corporate NOI market-implied cap rate" is closer to 4.57%.

From a Core FFO multiple perspective, AMH trades at 19.5x expected Core FFO, which again is on the high side for U.S. residential REITs. I do agree that relative to peers AMH will likely show an above-average Core FFO growth rate in 2025 but I expect performance to moderate going into 2026 as a key pillar of the robust 2025 performance - stronger occupancy - should not recur to the same extent in future years.

The company also employs relatively conservative leverage, with net debt only funding 25% of the REIT's enterprise value. I think this will hurt returns over the long term, although it does allow the company to borrow at very favorable terms (5.25% for 10-year debt).

All in all, taking account of my expectations for NOI growth to moderate in 2026, the high current valuation, and the conservative capital structure, I think AMH's common shares are currently a Hold.

The preferred shares

The company currently has two preferred issues outstanding - the 6.25% cumulative preferred shares (NYSE:AMH.PR.H) and the 5.875% cumulative preferred shares (NYSE:AMH.PR.G). The shares offer upside to par value of 5% and 9% respectively, with the current dividend yield quite identical at around 6.5%.

As indicated above preferred shares only account for 1% of enterprise value and enjoy a significant equity cushion. Likewise, preferred dividends take up only 2% of the expected 2025 core FFO, again underlining their high safety profile.

Considering the substantial equity cushion offered by the common stock and the insignificant amount preferred shares have in the REIT's capital structure, I would say the preferred shares have a risk only marginally higher than the company's debt. It is true that the preferred shares are perpetual, indicating they do carry some incremental duration risk. That said, the current risk premium relative to debt (cost of about 5.25% for 10-year maturity), at over 1%, appears excessive. For comparison, the difference in yields between 10-year and 30-year U.S. treasury bonds is only 0.25%.

Approaching the preferred shares from another angle, their 6.5% yield is only marginally below my circa 7.2% total return outlook for the company's common stock. The 7.2% total return outlook encompasses a 4.57% "corporate NOI" market-implied cap rate plus 2% inflation (in the long run), as well as a 0.6% boost from capital gains from the 25% of the enterprise value funded with preferred stock/debt. Considering the much lower risk of the preferred shares I think both preferred issues are worth a Buy rating.

Risks

As highlighted above, the preferred shares are very well covered whether we look at the common equity relative to preferred equity, or Core FFO relative to preferred dividends. That said, they do carry the risk of underperforming should long-term interest rates move higher (something I see as unlikely considering the Fed is still likely to cut interest rates over the next few years). The preferred shares will also do worse than the commons should inflation stabilize over 2% on a consistent basis.

Turning our attention to the common shares, we need to mention that occupancy may not recover as expected in 2025. This will negatively affect NOI growth and Core FFO growth which may lead to a lower valuation of AMH (since the stock currently trades at a premium thanks to robust NOI growth).

Investors will also pay attention as to whether the average change in rent for re-leases (defined by AMH as "the percentage change in annual rent on properties re-leased during the period, compared to the annual rent of the comparable long-term previous expired lease for each individual property") recovers to a rate closer to the change in rent for renewals (defined by AMH as "the percentage change in rent on non-month-to-month comparable long-term lease renewals during the period"). Rent for re-leases only grew 0.2% Y/Y in Q4 2024, well below the 4.9% increase in rent for renewals, resulting in a blended change in rent of 3.3% Y/Y for the quarter, the lowest over the past year:

Conclusion

American Homes 4 Rent delivered very strong Q4 2024 results, with Core FFO increasing 4.9% Y/Y thanks to robust NOI growth and better margins. Looking ahead to 2025, Core FFO is forecast to expand by only 3.3% to $1.83/share. The lower year-over-year increase is largely the result of moderation in NOI growth to about 3%, which is still a very healthy level for a residential REIT. I anticipate NOI growth to fall further in 2026 as the benefit from higher occupancy is unlikely to recur.

AMH's common shares trade at an elevated Core FFO multiple and a low market-implied cap rate, reflecting the REIT's strong NOI growth profile. I think the preferred shares offer better value relative to the common stock, offering a 6.5% current yield and some capital appreciation potential, notwithstanding risks stemming from a rise in long-term interest rates.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Accenture's GenAI Strategy Is Catching A Cold (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

It takes a lot to stay relevant in today’s business environment of GenAI-led businesses, which is evolving rapidly.

With innovation evolving at breakneck speed, companies have attempted to move quickly to match the competitive pace, while others buy their way into the competitive arena of GenAI.

So far, technology consulting heavyweight Accenture (NYSE:ACN) has been deploying its cash hoard into buying tech startups to bolster its GenAI offerings in the consulting domain of GenAI. This has helped the company with GenAI bookings, now accounting for ~4.5% of its booking backlog from zero a year ago.

But Accenture’s acquisition-led strategy comes with a cost that can turn around and slow down the company’s earnings growth and free cash growth if the acquisitions do not deliver the incremental delta to Accenture’s historical growth rate.

I have been optimistic about Accenture’s acquisition-led growth strategy over the past year but am moving to downgrade Accenture to a Neutral rating after my analysis projects a slower-than-expected growth rate in earnings this year.

Here’s How Accenture’s AI Strategy Has Worked In The Company’s Favor

Over the past year, my outlook on Accenture has been bullish since I believed the company’s acquisition-led strategy would elevate the company’s growth potential and help it stand out in the consulting domain of GenAI.

Specifically, I looked at the company’s New Bookings where I noted “strong signs of reacceleration in the company’s new bookings.”

As of Accenture’s FY25 Q1 earnings report, the company’s New Bookings rose 1% after demonstrating strong double-digit growth through FY24. Part of the reason for the growth spurt in bookings in FY24 was due to Accenture splashing cash worth “$6.6 billion across 46 acquisitions,” per management in the Q4 FY24 earnings call.

On a TTM basis, Accenture has now accumulated ~$82 billion worth of business bookings. ~54% of those bookings are related to the company’s Managed Services portfolio of services that continues to grow and account for a larger share in Accenture’s GenAI strategy. Also on a TTM basis, GenAI bookings have started accounting for a moderate share of Accenture business bookings with the company reporting ~$3.7 billion in GenAI bookings accounting for 4.5% of total TTM bookings.

For many quarters in the past few years, Accenture kept growing at an average pace of 2-3% y/y per quarter, with quarterly revenues of ~$15-$16 billion per quarter. As can be seen below, Accenture’s Q1 revenues surged by 8.5% y/y to $17.7 billion.

Note that the company reorganized its business geographies to the Americas, EMEA, and Asia Pacific, which now includes Latin America in its Americas region.

These results have given management enough confidence that their AI strategy is working, opening the door to 4-7% consolidated top-line growth in FY25 according to management’s last known projections. Inorganic growth, which is basically growth from acquisitions, is giving the company a 3% boost to top-line revenues. Without these acquisitions, Accenture would have continued to report low single-digit growth.

Here is how management portrayed their growth outlook:

For the full fiscal 2025, we now expect our revenue to be in the range of 4% to 7% growth in local currency over fiscal 2024, which includes an inorganic contribution of a bit more than 3%, which we expect will be about 4% in the first half and about 2% in the second half.

And we continue to expect to invest about $3 billion in acquisitions this fiscal year. For operating margin, we continue to expect fiscal year 2025 to be 15.6% to 15.8% at 10 to 30 basis point expansion over adjusted Fiscal 2024 results.

It makes sense why management still wants to deploy $3 billion in cash this year in following its acquisition-led strategy of recording higher growth. But these acquisitions need a higher payoff in the bottom line for the acquisitions to holistically make sense, especially with valuations at moderately rich levels.

Debt, Interest and The Cost Of Accenture’s AI Acquisitions

To fuel Accenture’s acquisition-led strategy, Accenture revealed last month that they raised $5 billion in debt, which now increases Accenture’s debt load to $8.1 billion versus cash+equivalents worth $8.3 billion.

It’s very easy to see that most of the debt will be used to finance its $3 billion acquisition plan for the year. But as noted by management in the previous section, 80-100 bps of GAAP operating margin expansion is not going to help its earnings grow at a rate where its valuation premium can be justified.

Let’s break down Accenture’s projected growth rate for FY25 and FY26 and compare its valuation.

For FY25, management projects ~4-7% growth in sales this year. Markets are slightly more optimistic about Accenture’s growth now projecting 6% growth in FY25 and 6.8% growth in FY26. Through FY25 and FY26, I have assumed a CAGR of 6.4% top-line growth.

Assuming 80-100 bps of margin expansion this year and next year, I estimate Accenture’s GAAP operating income to grow 13.1% over the next two fiscal years. This is quite impressive and demonstrates the proof in Accenture's AI strategy.

However, the additional debt that Accenture raised results in an additional ~$20 million interest expense per quarter in the current interest rate environment. With about ~$30 million in interest expenses incurred per quarter, the new debt issued means Accenture could spend ~$50 million per quarter or ~$2-$2.3 billion on an annualized basis. That is going to depress the operating income that investors can value, as I demonstrate in Accenture’s valuation model below.

Ideally, Accenture should be valued at 26-27x FY26 earnings due to the 13.1% CAGR in GAAP operating earnings over the next two years if I compare Accenture’s earnings growth to the S&P 500’s valuation of ~20x for 10% CAGR. However, after accounting for ~$2 billion in annual interest expenses, Accenture’s forward PE will drop to ~24x. This leads us to a PT of ~$404 for Accenture, implying the stock is fairly priced at the moment.

The valuation model above assumes a discount rate of 8.4% and share dilution rate of (0.5%). My assumption for the share dilution rate is based on the post-pandemic share buyback history and has taken into account management's outlook on share dilution.

Takeaway

Accenture’s GenAI strategy has led to the company seeing a strong spurt in the growth of its revenues. Acquiring AI companies has led to the company expecting 4-7% growth, up from the ~3% growth seen in the past two years.

But the recent debt raise means Accenture will incur more interest expenses, depressing earnings growth potential in the interim period. I am confident Accenture’s management will be able to eventually raise growth rates to justify its current debt load and interest expenses, but for now, the stock appears to be fairly valued.

I am downgrading Accenture to a Hold.

data.title: HYLD: A Fund Of Funds With Options
data.description:
All values are in CAD unless noted otherwise.

Covered calls serve to the purpose of supplementing monthly income, while at the same time reducing the investor's exposure to volatility. Of course, its not always a win (pocket monthly income) - win (shield from volatility) situation and sometimes the premium just stems some of the bleed if the underlying stock tanks. A lot of retail investors choose to invest in exchange traded funds that employ the covered call strategy, rather than pick and choose the stocks by themselves. We are not fans of funds that engage in passive or blind covered call writing i.e. they just replicate the benchmark index. No effort is made to navigate the market conditions and the fortune of those funds follows the index. Sure, in a prolonged bullish phase, all is well, but under "normal" market conditions, actively managed ETFs like JP Morgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) rise to the top. While we have never been buyers of this ETF, we have covered it quite a few times on this platform and we also reviewed its Canadian counterpart recently. Today we will cover another Canadian ETF, unrelated to the JPMorgan family, but one that also sells covered calls, well, indirectly.

The Fund

Started in February 2022, Hamilton Enhanced U.S. Covered Call ETF (TSX:HYLD:CA) endeavors to provide a "attractive monthly income, along with long-term capital appreciation" to its unitholders. It does so by investing its money ($834 million at last count) in other ETFs within the same fund family.

Yes, the sum of the above allocations exceeds 100% and that is because the HYLD uses leverage.

HYLD uses leverage in order to seek to achieve its investment objective. Leverage is created through the use of cash borrowings or as otherwise permitted under applicable securities legislation. The maximum aggregate leverage of HYLD will not exceed approximately 25% of its net asset value.

...

In order to ensure that a unitholder’s risk is limited to the capital invested, the ETF’s leverage is rebalanced in certain circumstances and when the leverage breaches certain bands. Specifically, the ETF’s leverage ratio is rebalanced back to 1.25x of the ETF’s NAV within two business days of the ETF’s leverage being less than 1.23x or greater than 1.27x.

Source: Interim Financial Report - June 30 2024

We get a broad idea of the sector allocations with the Holdings picture presented above. Once we factor in the US Equity ETF and account for leverage, Technology comes out on the top with close to 30% of the portfolio invested in it, followed by Healthcare and Financials.

The portfolio manager follows the S&P 500 for sector concentrations and that is noted as one of the highlights of the fund.

With the index having no ceiling at the moment, making it a highlight is a no brainer. HYLD has both, hedged and unhedged units as noted above. We are reviewing the former today. The hedged version is suitable for investors with a bullish to neutral view on the Canadian dollar.

All of the HYLD's holdings (Dec 31 holdings snapshot presented earlier in this piece) sell covered calls to augment income and reduce volatility. For example, Hamilton Technology Yield Maximizer ETF (QMAX:CA) notes this under its investment objective.

Each of them have a version of this under their investment objectives on their respective pages on the Hamilton ETFs website. Unlike HYLD, none of them use leverage currently. The fund writes options on only a portion of its holdings, but because it uses near term options, it gets a decent annualized yield out of it. Below we show the information on U.S. Equity YIELD MAXIMIZER™ ETF (SMAX:CA), its largest fund holding.

Expenses

Being a fund of funds, a proportionate share of the underlying fund expenses is passed on to HYLD and is included in its annual expense ratio. This along with its own annual expenses adds up to a hefty fee.

The 2.37% was after the top fund management fee was eliminated in early 2024.

...on January 29, 2024, the Manager announced a permanent reduction in the ETF’s management fee to 0.00% of the net asset value of the ETF as of February 7, 2024. The reduction follows the Manager’s plan to internalize all of the holdings of the ETF.

Source: Interim Financial Report - June 30 2024

So now HYLD investors get a break on direct management fees, but they still pay it indirectly as further explained in the financial report,

While the ETF will no longer pay fees directly to the Manager, the other ETFs managed by the Manager, in which the ETF will now invest, will continue to pay their applicable management fees to the Manager. As a result, the ETF and its investors will be indirectly subject to the management fees of the other ETFs.

Source: Interim Financial Report - June 30 2024

Distributions and Performance

HYLD distributes 14.30 cents on a monthly basis and currently yields a little over 12% (market price at the time of writing this piece $14.19).

This fund is actively managed, wherein the in,

The Manager reviews the portfolio holdings on an ongoing basis for possible additions, removals, or substitutions that in its discretion would be beneficial to HYLD and its Unitholders.

Source: Prospectus

In the three years this fund has existed, it has returned over 26% to its investors, which includes the distributions. Since it broadly tracks the S&P 500 Index, the trajectory is similar, just a bit muted for the ETF due to the hefty annual expenses.

The covered call selling strategy of its underlyings, while it shields against volatility, it also limits the capital appreciation. Still 26% is not shoddy at all, and while we would love to give all the credit to to the management for the double digit positive returns, having S&P 500 as a high flying beacon played a role in no small part. HYLD beat Global X S&P 500 Covered Call ETF (XYLD) during this period, despite the former having considerably lower annual expenses (0.60%). XYLD is a passive ETF and that uses the same index for its covered call writing strategy. The difference was of course, leverage.

Verdict

The ETF got a taste of the bear market in the year it started (2022) and it did not look pretty. The outperformance relative to XYLD can definitely be attributed to leverage. Our real criteria for things we want to buy is how they do in bear markets. It is here that the leverage hurt and the active management did not help. The fund lagged both SPY and XYLD during 2022 and by quite a huge margin.

The problem for most of these funds is that they get whipsawed with near term call options. In general, we prefer writing call options with an average duration of six months and will often use options two years out as well. This approach reduces volatility and whipsaws. We also actively avoid the index as that continues to get more bloated on valuations via passive flows. From these valuations, real (after inflation) returns are about negative 3% annually after dividends over the next decade.

For these reasons, we really cannot recommend HYLD, but it might be a good option for those that believe the US bull market is just getting started.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Unity Surges On Mixed Q4 Report: Here's Why The Bulls Are Right
data.description:
Unity Software Inc. (NYSE:U) reported its earnings on Thursday and provoked quite the market reaction.

Shares fell double-digits in pre-market immediately following the news release, hitting a low of $19. During the conference call, however, the mood abruptly changed, with Unity reversing its losses and rallying sharply.

By the end of Thursday's trading session, shares had surged 30% higher, closing the day at $28. Incredibly enough, in total, the stock rallied more than 45% off its pre-market lows.

Zooming out a bit, I believe this earnings report confirms that Unity shares reached their lows back in mid-2024 and are now establishing a new uptrend:

Let's unpack the company's earnings report and conference call and get to the root of what's causing the market's reaction. As a reminder, I wrote up my bullish thesis for Unity shares in December, and I'd refer back to that for a broader framework around the company. Today's article covers changes to the thesis after that point.

Q4: Huge Earnings Beat, Weak Guidance

Unity's Q4 earnings report had something for both the bulls and the bears. The positives were in the Q4 figures, which were a pleasant surprise. GAAP earnings of -$0.30 were seven cents ahead of expectations. Revenues of $457 million came in way ahead of the $433 million consensus.

Keep in mind that Unity was winding down its non-core businesses last year as it sought to cut costs and refocus on its operational strengths. This quarter, that began to pay off, as the company's core strategic portfolio returned to revenue growth. Strategic revenues rose 4% year-over-year to $442 million.

While Unity continued to report sharply lower year-over-year revenue results in Q4, that was entirely due to its decision to stop supporting most of its side businesses. As its non-strategic revenues were just $15 million in Q4, however, this wind-down period has now effectively ended. Almost the entirety of the company's revenues are now in its strategic businesses, which have returned to growth.

I'd like to highlight that Unity is now growing its primary business while it is engaged in massive headcount reductions. Judging from feedback on my prior article, I think this is a point that some investors are missing.

As of December 31st, 2019, the last year before the IPO, Unity had 2,734 full-time employees. This ballooned to roughly 8,000 employees at its peak, both from aggressive internal hiring and the acquisitions of firms such as IronSource and Weta Digital. The actual revenues from Unity's game engine business couldn't scale at nearly the same rate as it was hiring people, however, and the IronSource acquisition failed to jumpstart the mobile ads business nearly as much as expected.

So, the company ended up with a headcount that was exceeding what the company's cash flows could support and also far beyond what was necessary to run the company's game engine and ads business. Unity was working on a lot of cool side projects and things that could have become major revenue generators 5–10 years into the future, but the core business wasn't scaling enough to fund it all while maintaining profitability.

Unity announced yet more layoffs in February 2025, following last year's aggressive headcount reductions. Some traders reacted to that negatively, thinking that it must mean trouble. I'd suggest a different interpretation, however, in that the company is still far above the 2,734 employees it had in 2019 when it was already nearly ready to go public and enjoying rapid growth. We can debate the exact amount of workforce the company needs, but I'm convinced it can operate the core strategic businesses capably with a much leaner employee base.

This brings us to the matter of guidance. Unity set its Q1 guidance very low, with both adjusted EBITDA and revenue midpoints coming in well below consensus. For example, Unity is guiding to a midpoint of $410 million in revenues, compared to the street's consensus estimate of $440 million.

How worried should we be about this?

Management Seemingly Set Low Expectations For Q1 Intentionally

In isolation, the weak guidance might seem like a serious red flag. Traders understandably took a cautious approach immediately upon seeing the earnings release, briefly driving Unity stock below $20 per share as an initial reaction.

That said, there is more context to be considered. For one thing, in the just-concluded Q4, Unity beat the top end of its prior adjusted guidance by a full 26%. The point being that the new CEO is comfortable setting conservative guidance numbers. I'd further add that Unity's strategic revenues rose sequentially in Q4, up to $457 million. Given that core revenues are growing again and are already above $450 million, guidance of $410 million for Q1 revenues seems like a very low bar for the company to meet.

Adding color to that, CEO Matthew Bromberg explained why the company set such a seemingly low guide for Q1 in the conference call. Specifically, here is Bromberg discussing the company's advertising business and its upcoming switchover to a new AI ads platform:

"We firmly believe that we have the assets and the capabilities to grow [our ad business] much faster, and so today we’re excited to officially announce the migration of the Unity ad network to our new AI platform, which we’re calling Unity Vector. The migration begins towards the end of Q1 with this first phase of work slated to be complete by the end of Q2 2025. Ongoing efforts to expand the scale and our quality of our offering will of course continue thereafter [...]

Unity Vector is a significant undertaking and we won’t see the benefits immediately. The caution in our Q1 guide reflects some prudence with respect to this."

It's understandable that a large platform migration like this will take some time, and it's reasonable to think that there could be some temporary disruption in business flow while this migration is under way. So, in this context, Unity's cautious Q1 guidance is sensible. Until the platform migration is complete and advertisers start to see the benefits of the enhanced data integration, it will be difficult to forecast the exact quarter-to-quarter fluctuations in ad activity.

In addition, Unity's current CEO assumed the role in May 2024, and the new CFO, Jarrod Yahes, took over in January 2025. This is a new management team that is still getting its bearings. Unity just launched its Unity 6 engine update in October, and Unity Vector is rolling out this year. With all these moving pieces, it makes sense for management to set a low guidance bar and leave more room to exceed expectations going forward.

Unity Stock Verdict

This earnings report confirms my prior outlook on Unity, namely that the company's transformation is on track and shares are significantly undervalued compared to peers.

After my previous article, shares of rival mobile games advertising business AppLovin (NASDAQ:APP) skyrocketed on upbeat earnings results.

These are not directly comparable businesses. Unity has its graphics engine related revenues, while AppLovin is more of a pureplay ad business. AppLovin is also growing at a massive rate, while Unity has not been, at least not recently. That said, AppLovin recently hit 40x enterprise value-to-sales and 75x enterprise value-to-EBITDA. That's a massive gap compared to Unity, which was around 5x sales before Thursday.

The case for further convergence of their respective multiples grows stronger when considering that Unity hired Jim Payne as its Chief Product Officer in August 2024. Payne, for those unfamiliar, was the co-founder of both MAX and MoPub. AppLovin would ultimately acquire both MAX and MoPub to serve as major pieces of its mobile games and e-commerce advertising platform.

Now, the thinking is, Payne can build similar services for Unity and help the company wrest market share from AppLovin in this fast-growing space.

And with the results from Q4 and the launch of the new Unity Vector AI ad platform, there is growing reason to think Payne will be able to deliver on this potential. Given the massive disparity between the valuations of AppLovin and Unity, if and when Unity's mobile ads gain more competitive parity with AppLovin, that could lead to a major re-rating in U's share price. Then Thursday's Unity Software stock rally could be just the opening salvo.

data.title: Amazon's Next Big Profit Driver
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) has surged past the $220 target since my last coverage, delivering a +25% return — more than double the S&P 500’s gain. The company’s strength lies in its ability to drive revenue growth while boosting profitability, with its high-margin ad business and record third-party sales fueling expansion. Prime membership growth, logistics optimizations, and automation are cutting costs and reinforcing its competitive edge. With e-commerce dominance and multiple growth levers, Amazon remains a compelling long-term bet, making any pullbacks a buying opportunity.

Stronger Top-line With Profitability Uplifts Possible in CY25

Amazon generated $187.8B in revenue for Q,4-24, marking a 10% YoY increase. This growth is vital under the $700M FX headwind that impacted the quarter. Without this headwind, top-line growth would have been 11%, exceeding the top end of the company’s guidance. This indicates that the company can grow top-line despite adverse FX movements through the strength of its diversified business model. Here, Amazon’s ad business continues to be a growth yielder. The ad business has derived $17.3B in revenue in Q4 with an 18% YoY jump outpacing the consolidated growth, equating to an annual revenue run rate of $69B, 2X the $29B reported four years ago. Sponsored products account for the largest portion of ad revenue, and Amazon sees further growth opportunities in this segment along with the retail business.

Amazon’s Stores business (North America and International segments) had solid growth. The North America segment derived a 10% YoY top-line jump. Whereas the international segment grew by 9% if we exclude FX impacts. This growth was based on Amazon’s moves to expand its product selection pool, lower prices, and improve user convenience. The company’s platform added brands like Clinique, Estee Lauder (EL), Oura Rings, and Armani Beauty. This boosts Amazon.com’s product assortment and attracts a larger customer base.

Third-party sellers now accounted for 61% of the items sold on Amazon in CY24. This is the highest annual mix ever. This shift towards a higher proportion of third-party sales is a boost for Amazon. Why? It reduces the company’s inventory risk and capital requirements and increases the platform’s attractiveness due to its vast selection. The launch of Amazon Haul in Q4 (which offers ultra-low-priced products in one convenient destination) has also been well-received and has led to strong unit growth that outpaced top-line growth.

Further, Amazon’s pricing strategy continues to be a vital advantage. In Q4, consumers saved over $15B through Amazon’s low everyday prices and promotional events (like Prime Big Deal Days, Black Friday, and Cyber Monday). As per the Federal annual pricing study, Amazon had the lowest online prices for the eighth consecutive year. It is averaging 14% lower than other leading US retailers. This pricing advantage plus fast and reliable delivery reinforces Amazon’s lead in the market.

Moving to Amazon Prime, in CY24, Amazon expanded its same-day delivery sites by over 60%, now serving over 140 metro areas. The company delivered +9B units the same or the next day worldwide (setting a new record). This focus on delivery speed and convenience is driving accelerated growth in Prime membership and may continue to push up Amazon’s top-line growth.

As of now, Amazon Prime provides comprehensive benefits for $14.99/month. With that, Prime members can have unlimited free shipping on 300M items (often with same-day/one-day delivery) and access to exclusive shopping events (like Prime Day), premium programming and live sports (Prime Video), ad-free listening of 100M songs and podcasts with Amazon Music, and unlimited grocery delivery from Whole Foods Market and Amazon Fresh (for $9.99/month). Also, Prime members benefit from a free Grubhub+ membership and a $0.10/gallon fuel discount at BP, Amoco, and AMPM stations. So, in short, the value proposition of Prime is compelling against competitor membership services that offer fewer benefits at higher prices.

At the bottom line, the regionalization of the US fulfillment network and the redesign of the inbound network have improved inventory placement (bringing more items closer to customers). Ahead of Black Friday, Amazon improved the percentage of ordered units available in the ideal building by over 40% YoY. This optimization reduces delivery times and lowers expenses. The company has also optimized the number of items sent in the same package (reducing packaging waste and transportation costs). Per-unit transportation costs continue to decline as Amazon builds out and optimizes its last-mile network. These base efficiencies have led to a reduction in the global cost of serving on a per-unit basis for the second consecutive year.

Looking ahead to CY25, Amazon may continue to reduce costs through better inventory placement, expansion of the same-day delivery network, and increased use of robotics + automation. These may derive continued improvements in operating margins under an expanding top line. The street is also expecting continued similar top-line growth in CY25 and CY26. In short, revenue expansion is becoming more profitable, and this will benefit the stock’s valuations in the mid-term. Now, considering the top-line valuation and growth premiums over the sector median, the stock is fairly valued, and any correction in the stock may serve as an entry point for bulls.

Amazon’s Growing Risks: Logistics, Automation, and Ad Growth Under Pressure

Amazon’s dependence on third-party logistics partners (particularly UPS) presents a business-side risk. However, Amazon has increasingly shifted its shipments to its own logistics network to reduce its dependence. This move has allowed Amazon to scale up its operations with control over delivery speed (and cost). UPS has decided to exit from 50% of the volume it previously handled for Amazon due to lower margins. This transition places additional pressure on Amazon’s internal logistics capabilities. It requires the company to handle a larger share of its shipments independently. Amazon has managed this shift so far, but any disruptions in its logistics network (due to capacity, labor issues, or operational inefficiencies) can hit hard on delivery performance.

Also, Amazon’s investment in robotics and automation has seen cost savings and productivity improvements, but scaling the tech across its massive fulfillment network presents a scalability issue. The recent integration of new robotics initiatives in the Shreveport facility has shown positives, but it is still in the early stages. Expanding the upgrades to other facilities may require massive time and capital investment. Any delays or failures in the deployment of robotics could cap the reduction of its cost to serve and delivery speeds.

Finally, Amazon’s ad business is not without its issues. The ad market is highly competitive, with major leads by Google (GOOG), Facebook (META), and other emerging platforms drilling for market share. For Amazon to build its ad business, top-line growth depends on its level of investment in order to differentiate its ad portfolio. The company has led in full-funnel ads and leveraging client data for targeted campaigns, but it faces increasing scrutiny over data privacy. Any shift in data privacy regulations towards targeted ads can crack Amazon’s sharpness in monetizing its ad platform. Lastly, as the ad business grows on an increasingly large base, maintaining high growth rates will become more challenging and may lead to slower top-line ad growth. This can cause sharp hits on the stock.

Takeaway

Amazon continues to dominate e-commerce while expanding its high-margin ad business and optimizing logistics for profitability. Its strategic investments in Prime, automation, and supply chain efficiency position it for sustained growth. While scaling challenges and competition pose risks, Amazon’s ability to execute on its strengths makes it a compelling long-term opportunity.

data.title: Birkenstock Adds Winter Assortment, But The Stock Remains Too Expensive
data.description:
Birkenstock Holding plc (NYSE:BIRK) reported Q1 2025 results, with revenue rising 19% year-over-year.

Still, the market reacted negatively as the company maintained its full-year guidance at 15%-17% growth, signaling a potential deceleration from 20% last year.

Another concern was the composition of growth: wholesale outperformed (+30%) while DTC slowed to +10% (vs. +30% last year). Additionally, wholesale expansion was largely driven by increased assortment at existing doors (90% of growth), raising concerns about saturation.

On the positive, there were good signs of product diversification—closed-toe silhouettes accounted for 50% of revenue this quarter.

Still, the company’s valuation remains extreme at 27x next-twelve-months (NTM) earnings, considering the large fashion risk and revenue deceleration signs.

Recap: a great brand, but high fashion risk

My original Birkenstock article is from July 2024, and I rated it a Hold. Back then, I considered the company to have a strong franchise, but it was nonetheless largely a single-product, high-fashion risk brand. Trading at nearly 30x earnings, it required flawless execution and sustained high growth to justify its valuation. So far, the stock is down despite posting 20/15% growth, giving some credit to my concerns.

Q1 2025 results

Strong growth but decelerating and lower quality?: Revenues were up 19% YOY in the company's slowest quarter (not that many people buy sandals during the Northern Hemisphere winter).

This should be a good figure, but there are also some more concerning points regarding growth quality.

First, the company maintained a 15/17% yearly guidance, below last year (20%) and below 1Q25. Second, DTC was significantly slower than the whole company, at +10% YOY (management said the comp was hard in 1Q24 at 30% YOY).

Third, wholesale was the main driver (+30%), which is generally good, but at least 90% of growth (acc. to management) came from assorting the same doors. This is not bad, but it may not be something that is repeatable, given Birkenstock is expanding towards winter-oriented sales mostly. Management said that wholesale and DTC growth rates should meet across the year.

Management claims to maintain strict assortment discipline, citing a 40% sell-through increase. However, this is somewhat at odds with the slowdown in DTC growth.

Very good signs of product diversification: A notable highlight from the quarter was Birkenstock’s growing success in diversifying its product mix beyond sandals. This was the main risk I was concerned with since I started covering the name.

Closed-toe silhouettes made up 50% of revenue in 1Q25. In part, this was driven by assortment expansion at wholesalers, and also by the winter in the Northern Hemisphere, but still a strong signal that the brand is moving beyond its traditional image.

Of the 600 basis points gained by closed-toe products, 300bps came from lace-up shoes and boots, marking a potentially significant expansion into a new category. While it remains to be seen whether this trend will be sustained throughout the year, it is a step in the right direction.

Opening in Asia: Asia grew 47% YOY, adding 3/4% of sales growth (it represents 13% of sales versus 10% last year). Here, wholesale is also key, with mono-brand partner stores. Still a small market, but that could easily represent another Europe or America.

Valuation remains too optimistic

Birkenstock Holding plc stock remains priced for perfection.

Based on the company’s full-year guidance, revenues should be $2.3 billion (+15%-17% FX-neutral growth). Adjusted EBITDA margin was guided at 31%, resulting in adjusted EBITDA of $713 million. Removing $100 million in D&A and $100 million in interest, plus 25% in taxes, results in FY25 net income of $387 million.

Compared with a market cap of $10.5 billion, this is a P/E ratio of 27x. Such a P/E requires A LOT of growth to make sense, which Birkenstock could achieve, but the problem is that the price already discounts it. For example, even if Birkenstock sustains 15% annual growth for six years without taking on new debt, its valuation in 2030 would still be ~13x earnings.

The positive future is already discounted, and the risks (deceleration, fashion risk, consumer discretionary) are ignored.

BIRK is the case of a great company but not a great stock. I maintain my Hold on the name.

data.title: Palantir: The Math Doesn't Work For Me
data.description:
Description

Palantir Technologies (NASDAQ:PLTR) was founded in May 2003 by Alex Karp and Peter Thiel, among others. Some of the first investors included the U.S. Central Intelligence Agency and Peter Thiel's venture capital firm, Founders Fund. From its beginnings, Palantir has been intimately connected with some of the most powerful intelligence agencies in the Western world. As discussed by Alex Karp in some interviews, the firm has been playing a critical role not only in the conflict between Ukraine and Russia but pretty much all around the world, including in the Middle East and Taiwan. The fact that Alex Karp was the first Western CEO to visit Volodymyr Zelensky after Russia's invasion of Ukraine in February 2022 illustrates the firm's mission-critical offerings.

The deteriorating conditions in the Middle East are slowly putting the Strait of Hormuz into focus. Even though this route is absolutely vital for oil trade, it is less so for containership. In my view, the real deal when it comes to geopolitical risk is the Taiwan Strait, and if Hormuz is the world's jugular vein for oil, the Taiwan Strait and the South China Sea are the jugulars for containerships. Danaos Corporation: Providing A Long-Volatility Exposure To Geopolitics

Palantir is at the intersection of artificial intelligence and geopolitics, two of the most powerful trends affecting the world today. The AI theme has been one of the most impactful on equity markets over the last quarters, and Palantir has been one of the key beneficiaries in this regard. When it comes to geopolitics, tensions between China and Taiwan continue to increase. For example, China simulated a sea and air blockade of Taiwan in October 2024, an additional sign of mounting tensions in the region. With an ongoing AI race and an increasingly unstable world from a geopolitical perspective, the current euphoria reflected by the stock's meteoric rise is understandable in a way. How many companies can pretend to sit at the intersection point between some of the most powerful forces shaping Western civilization today?

Before discussing valuation, I want to acknowledge the validity of the optimism surrounding the company. However, from a psychological point of view, one risk associated with optimism is that it becomes detached from reality and morphs into euphoria. Looking back on my personal journey from downtown Toronto to a yurt in the woods, I realize that sustained optimism, kept in check by real and reasonable challenges, is the strategy that I unconsciously adopted. If my journey would have been characterized by bouts of euphoria followed by major disappointment, I don't think I would have made it. In a sense, the euphoria of today is simply the polar opposite of the panic of tomorrow. How long before there is a shift in polarity? One thing I have learned from people suffering from bipolar disorder is that it can happen extremely quickly following a completely unexpected trigger. If stock prices are ruled by sentiment in the short term, I think psychology may offer some useful guidelines.

Palantir may also be one of the most well-connected companies in the world. It is now well-known that Palantir, Peter Thiel, and multiple intelligence agencies have had close relationships for many years and even decades in some cases.

In turn, Peter Thiel was a key fund provider for Trump's election campaign. Inside the Trump administration, the link between Peter Thiel, Elon Musk, and David Sacks is also apparent as they are part of the infamous PayPal Mafia. As Forbes mentioned in July 2024, the relationship between Thiel and JD Vance, the 50th Vice President of the United States under the Trump administration, goes back to 2011. Moreover, there probably was not another time in history when the CEO of a major corporation was so close publicly with a President. From this perspective, the relationship between Elon Musk and Donald Trump is quite unique.

Palantir was listed on the NYSE at $10.00 per share in September 2020. Based on the 2.17 billion diluted shares outstanding at the time, it was equivalent to a market capitalization of ~$21.7 billion. In contrast, Palantir was valued at $20 billion in December 2015 following a $880 million funding round. Until May 2023, Palantir's stock was still trading below the $10.00 threshold. In fact, it was only when Trump's election campaign started to gain momentum in the summer of 2024 that the share price took off and went parabolic. With 2.5 billion fully diluted shares outstanding and a price of around $110.00 per share, we are now talking about a market capitalization of ~$275 billion. Despite generating less than $3.0 billion in revenues over the course of 2024, Palantir is now the 31st largest component in the S&P 500, with a weighting of 0.47%. When the company was added to the S&P 500 towards the end of September 2024, the share price was still hovering around $35.00.

As discussed in a previous article about MicroStrategy (MSTR), large inflows from passive funds can trigger self-reinforcing feedback loops to the upside, especially when the reflexive impacts of hedging activities by option market markers are taken into account. In this sense, market mechanics triggered by the S&P 500 inclusion and further fueled by Trump's campaign seem to be another factor leading to the perfect storm to the upside we have witnessed over the last few months.

In today's market ecosystem, feedback loops have become increasingly important in understanding the behaviour of market prices. The short and gamma squeezes on Tesla following the pandemic is another example. On top, Tesla's share price was also fueled by its inclusion in the S&P 500 in December 2020. Those three dynamics were self-reinforcing, and the consequence was a historic rally in the share price. Even though there was a large crowd of fundamental investors screaming about overvaluation, it did not matter to market mechanics. MicroStrategy: Feedback Loops Also Work In Reverse

Valuation

As disclosed in the Q4-24 conference call, Palantir expects revenues of ~$3.75 billion for the full year 2025, the equivalent of a ~30% growth rate compared to the $2.87 billion reported in 2024. As indicated in the chart below, Palantir's revenue growth rate has been accelerating since the second quarter of 2023. For example, the revenue growth of 36.0% reported in the fourth quarter represents a drastic acceleration versus the 19.6% growth reported in Q4-23. Using the Q1-25 revenue guidance of $860 million provided by management, Palantir could repeat the impressive financial results delivered in Q4-24. In my view, accelerating growth is another key element in understanding the recent pop in the share price. To justify the current valuation, revenue growth must continue to increase. In fact, simply maintaining a 30% revenue growth rate over multiple years would be insufficient to support the current market capitalization.

Using the growth curve illustrated below, it would take Palantir slightly more than 11 years to generate revenues equivalent to its current ~$275 billion market capitalization. Using a simple price-to-sales ratio based on 2024 revenues, Palantir currently trades at more than 95x revenues, one of the highest multiples I have ever seen for a company of this size. Using the 2025 revenue guidance of $3.75 billion, we are talking a price-to-sales multiple of ~73x. Scott McNealy, the former CEO of Sun Microsystems, made history with his speech regarding stocks trading at 10x revenues during the dot-com bubble. Although I fully recognize Palantir's massive growth opportunities, the current AI boom seems to be producing companies trading at multiple that Scott McNealy could probably have never imagined.

At 10 times revenues, to give you a 10-year payback, I have to pay you 100% of revenues for 10 straight years in dividends. That assumes I can get that by my shareholders. That assumes I have zero cost of goods sold, which is very hard for a computer company. That assumes zero expenses, which is really hard with 39,000 employees. That assumes I pay no taxes, which is very hard. And that assumes you pay no taxes on your dividends, which is kind of illegal. And that assumes with zero R&D for the next 10 years, I can maintain the current revenue run rate. Now, having done that, would any of you like to buy my stock at $64? Do you realize how ridiculous those basic assumptions are? You don’t need any transparency. You don’t need any footnotes. What were you thinking? Scott McNealy, Sun Microsystems

In my forecast, I used stable gross margins of 80%, which are in line with what Palantir reported over the last three years. Could gross margins increase further over time? It is possible, but 80% gross margins are some of the highest I am aware of, and maintaining those levels over the long run would be an achievement in itself.

Since 2019, Palantir has made significant progress in amortizing sales and marketing expenses, as well as general and administrative expenses. Once combined, those two line items represented 103.8% of revenues in 2019 compared to 51.7% in 2024. This is where the power of operating leverage comes into play. The chart below illustrates the long-term assumption used in my model. A more optimistic scenario would assume a faster amortization, mostly as a consequence of faster revenue growth. Given Palantir's current position in its lifecycle, imposing strict control on operating expenses may not be the ideal strategy if it limits growth.

Over the last three years, Palantir reported research and development expenses representing approximately 18% of revenue. In my model, I assumed a gradual amortization of these expenses until they reached 5.0% of revenues in 2041. Of course, nobody knows what the true figures will look like, but the key point is that Palantir's free cash flow is likely to increase quite dramatically in the coming years as the company has now reached a critical size.

Using the assumptions discussed above, it would take almost 15 years for Palantir to generate operating income equivalent to its current market capitalization of $275 billion. Market participants buying Palantir today are making implicit cash outlays of $275 billion in exchange for being entitled to the operating income moving forward. Under this scenario, an investor paying today's valuation would simply have recouped its capital by 2040; no return on capital would be earned yet.

If we include a return on capital of 10%, it would take a holding period of 22 years for Palantir to generate operating income equivalent to its current market capitalization. Of course, this is entirely dependent on the assumptions mentioned above. However, getting the thinking right is much more important than getting the various numbers precisely right. Those calculations reflect what the business could potentially earn on its own, irrespective of what is happening surrounding financial markets.

Considering the massive duration embedded in Palantir's $275 billion market capitalization, many things can go wrong before an investor can recoup his capital intrinsically through the business's operation and growth. Evidently, almost nobody would actually own the stock for such a long period of time, meaning that people buying the stock today are entirely dependent on finding another buyer at a higher price. Simply being patient and getting out of a hole through the intrinsic growth of the business would be vastly insufficient if market sentiment were to change.

Conclusion

Over the last few hours, the Trump administration ordered the Pentagon to develop plans to reduce defence spending by 8% in each of the next five years. Considering the leading-edge nature of Palantir's products, I doubt the company would be impacted by a reduction in defence spending, but the impact on market sentiment has been immediate.

Just a few hours earlier, Alex Karp also announced plans to sell $1.2 billion in common stock. According to Barron's, Palantir's CEO sold 40.7 million shares in 2024 at an average price of $47.99 per share. It is another element that could indicate the beginning of a sentiment shift. For the stock price to become under pressure, a mere reduction in euphoria would be sufficient enough.

Using the assumptions detailed in this article, investors buying the stock today would have to wait ~22 years before earning an annual return on capital of 10%. An extremely long payback period makes the stock particularly vulnerable to a shift in sentiment.

For disclosure purposes, I have a long position in the Direxion Daily PLTR Bear 1X Shares (PLTD) ETF. Although inverse ETFs are subject to beta slippage, they provide maximum downside risk in return due to their daily rebalancing mechanism. As described in my previous short articles about MicroStrategy and Tesla, extreme prudence is required in this kind of situation. I have now closed profitably my short position on Tesla and MicroStrategy. This capital has been redeployed to a short Palantir position via PLTD.

data.title: Uniti Group Surges As The Bullish Case Becomes Obvious
data.description:
February 21st ended up being a really exciting day for shareholders of Uniti Group Inc. (NASDAQ:UNIT). At one point, shares of the company were up as much as 10.3%. This came after news that the company reported financial results for the final quarter of its 2024 fiscal year and offered up guidance for 2025. Considering that it's one of my largest holdings at this time, it stands to reason that I was a very happy camper with this development. But even with this surge, I maintain that shares are drastically undervalued. Even though the company fell short of analysts’ expectations, the overall picture for the business is positive, and I do believe that the company deserves the “strong buy” rating that I have had for some time.

So far, my bullish stance on the company has played out well. Since I last reaffirmed it as a “strong buy” candidate back in October of last year, shares are up a whopping 14.7%. That's well above the 6.3% upside achieved by the S&P 500 (SP500) over the same window of time. But that's nothing compared to performance over a longer window of time. Since I first upgraded the company to a “strong buy” in May 2024, shares have achieved an upside of 40.3%. That's massively above the 18.3% upside achieved by the broader market. But even with this kind of return, I maintain that further upside is appropriate. Because of this, I have decided to reaffirm the company as a ‘strong buy’ at this time.

The picture still looks strong

To be honest with you, the final quarter of the 2024 fiscal year was a bit mixed for Uniti Group. The business reported revenue of $293.3 million. That represents an increase of 2.7% compared to the $285.7 million reported the previous year. Having said that, revenue was still $2.3 million lower than what analysts were hoping to see. Digging into the data further, we can see that most of this upside came from its Uniti Leasing business, with sales up 3.1% from $214.9 million to $221.7 million. The overwhelming majority of this revenue comes from the firm’s relationship with Windstream. And that party is subject to small annual escalations in how much it's paying. Meanwhile, Uniti Fiber, which focuses on infrastructure solutions like cell site backhaul and small cell for wireless operators, not to mention other types of customers, experienced a 1.3% increase in revenue from $70.7 million to $71.7 million.

Even though revenue increased during this time, profitability actually worsened. Earnings per share contracted from $0.13 to $0.09, even missing analysts’ estimates by $0.01. This translated to a decline in net profits from $3.4 million to $21 million. Some of this was because of a smaller income tax benefit. However, the company also saw a surge in transaction-related costs from $2.8 million to $7.7 million. FFO (Funds from Operations) per share ended up falling slightly from $0.32 to $0.30, causing overall FFO to decline from $85.7 million to $77.9 million. But on an adjusted basis, it rose from $0.34 to $0.35. This still ended up being $0.01 lower than what analysts were hoping to see. However, it did translate to an increase in adjusted FFO from $91.6 million to $92 million.

The good news is that operating cash flow jumped from $162.5 million to $185.6 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a more modest increase from $102.5 million to $103.7 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company grew from $231.1 million to $239.5 million. To be honest with you, I consider these three cash flow metrics to be more important than net profits, FFO, or adjusted FFO.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for 2024 as a whole relative to 2023. Except for adjusted operating cash flow and adjusted FFO, the picture for the business improved across the board. Management expects further improvements for 2025. They currently believe that revenue will be between just under $1.20 billion and $1.22 billion. At the midpoint, this would represent an increase of 3.4% compared to the $1.17 billion the company reported for 2024. Net profits attributable to common shareholders should rise to between $95 million and $115 million. Even the low end of this is higher than the $91.3 million the company reported for 2024.

When it comes to guidance, management was actually quite detailed. In the image above, you can even see certain per-share estimates that the company has provided. But focusing on the big picture, we can also expect an FFO of between $322 million and $342 million compared to the $294.5 million reported for 2024 and an adjusted FFO of between $369 million and $389 million. That figure would be above the $358.9 million reported for last year. Lastly, EBITDA for the company should come in somewhere between $966 million and $986 million. For context, for 2024, that figure was only $940.1 million.

All things considered, the picture for the business is looking up. In addition to continuing to grow, the company is also making progress elsewhere. For instance, from the third quarter to the fourth quarter, management paid down $127.2 million worth of net debt. After the end of the quarter, the company ended up closing a $589 million note offering secured by some of the company's fiber assets. These carry with them a 6.5% weighted average interest rate. With the proceeds, the firm paid down $125 million of senior notes due in 2028 which had an annual interest rate of 10.50%. They also paid off $275 million from an asset-based credit facility that had a 10.33% interest rate.

We don't know what the company is planning to do with the remaining $189 million. I hope that they will pay off other debt as well. However, even though they only mentioned what they did with $400 million of the proceeds, the interest savings on that amounts to $38.3 million per year compared to the $41.5 million in interest expense the debt they paid off cost shareholders.

I also think it would be appropriate, at this time, to talk about its pending merger with Windstream. It was news of the two companies combining that caused me to upgrade the stock to a ‘strong buy’ even though shares initially plunged on the news. Every day, this decision looks a bit better than the day before. And that's difficult considering how good the development was in my view.

On February 20th, updated financial data for Windstream became public. Year over year, the company claimed that fiber coverage grew by 11%, resulting in 1.6 million consumer premises passed and with 37% of fiber coverage now in the firm’s Kinetic markets. The enterprise also grew fiber subscribers by 16% year over year. This brought the total number of fiber subscribers up to 446,000. The overall penetration rate for these customers is 27.4%. That compares favorably to the 26.3% that the company enjoyed the previous year. Furthermore, fiber subscriber revenue jumped from $321 million in 2023 to $408 million in 2024, driven in part by a rise in monthly fiber ARPU from $68.74 to $71.07.

Another positive thing about the company is that the market potential is significant. Management seems particularly interested in AI infrastructure. In the image below, you can see the magnitude of this market and how much it is slated to grow by the year 2030. Naturally, this offers the business a meaningful upside as it continues to make significant investments in growing its network.

For 2025, Windstream expects to grow the number of premises passed to roughly 2 million. And the number of consumer fiber subscribers is forecasted to hit around 550,000, driven not only by the network expansion, but also by an increase in the fiber penetration rate to 28%. Overall profitability for the company will probably remain largely unchanged from 2024 to 2025. For instance, EBITDAR (which is EBITDA before factoring out rents paid to Uniti Group) hit $1.46 billion in 2024. But for 2025, it is expected to be $1.4 billion. Actual EBITDA for the company was just under $1.01 billion last year. Using the data provided by management, which is only partial since Windstream is not a publicly traded company and does not need to provide every detail, I estimated that the adjusted operating cash flow was around $801 million.

In the table above, you can see certain projections regarding what profitability and valuations look like for both Uniti Group and Windstream based on these updated financial results. On an EV-to-EBITDA basis, Uniti Group is trading at a multiple of only 7.3. For Windstream, that number is 4.5. And on a combined basis, the multiple should be around 7.3 using estimates for 2025.

In the table below, you can see, using previous projections by management for what the combined company will look like by the year 2028, how the company should be valued. Even in the most conservative case provided by management, we should be looking at a fair value per share for the combined business of $12.28. Compared to the $5.94 that shares of Uniti Group are going for currently, this would mean an annualized upside of 19.9%. But using some of the more aggressive scenarios, we would be looking at an upside of as much as 51.5% per annum. This could take shares up to as much as $31.29.

Takeaway

Based on the data provided, I must say that I am pleased with how things are turning out. Even though it is one of my largest holdings, I honestly regret not buying more of it. Yes, management did fall short of expectations when it came to the top and bottom lines for the quarter. But the overall picture for shareholders looks positive, and I see no reason why, given the massive amount of upside that's on the table, that shares should be rated anything besides a “strong buy.”
